nativeRange.habitat: Steep outer reef slopes between 10 and 40 m, typically on the ceilings of caves, overhangs and ledges where individuals are often observed oriented upside down.

diet.notes: Wild diet is undocumented. In captivity, generally advised to offer a varied mix of Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp and other small meaty items. Multiple small feedings per day tend to work better than one or two large ones; established systems with grazable live rock can help reluctant feeders transition.

avoidWith: boisterous tangs or large angels that bully shy fish

summary: A rare, expert-only dwarf angelfish from deep western Pacific reef caves, marked by a vivid yellow belly and electric-blue dorsum. Shy and reclusive in the wild but reportedly assertive toward similarly-sized tankmates as it matures.
careNotes: The Purple Masked Angelfish is classified as expert-only and is rarely available in the trade, commanding very high prices when offered. Specimens are typically collected at 10-40 m and tend to acclimate poorly, so a well-established reef system with abundant live rock, deep caves and overhangs is generally advised — wild individuals are most often seen upside down on cave ceilings, and replicating that structural complexity helps reduce stress. Subdued lighting or a deeply shaded section of rockwork helps shy specimens feed and settle. Reef compatibility is rated 'with caution' by LiveAquaria; isolated cases of nipping at sessile invertebrates, LPS coral polyps and clam mantles have been reported. Tankmate selection matters: pair only with calm, similarly-sized or smaller fish that will not outcompete it at feeding time. Genus placement is unsettled — some authorities use Paracentropyge venusta (subgenus elevated to genus), while FishBase and most modern catalogs retain Centropyge venusta; the species was originally described as Holacanthus venustus (Yasuda & Tominaga, 1969). Like other dwarf angelfish, the species is generally considered a protogynous hermaphrodite and may form loose harems on the reef, so a single individual or a carefully introduced female-then-male pair is the safest captive configuration. IUCN Red List status is Least Concern. Cross-source notes: LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 70 US gal (~265 L); we set 300 L as a conservative cautious-larger figure given the species' shy temperament, deep-cave biotope and reported need for ample rockwork.
breedingNotes: Sporadically bred and reared in captivity, but the species remains exceptionally rare in the hobby and pelagic-spawning protocols are not well documented for hobbyist-scale systems. Like related dwarf angelfish, individuals are believed to be protogynous hermaphrodites that form harems with one dominant male over multiple females. Natural hybrids with Centropyge multifasciata have been reported on the reef.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of aquarium husbandry parameters (tank size, temperament, reef compatibility, diet, water parameters). Uses the name Paracentropyge venusta.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm TL — agrees with LiveAquaria's 5 in / ~12.7 cm), taxonomy (family Pomacanthidae, order Acanthuriformes), depth range (10-40 m), native distribution (Japan to northern Philippines), habitat (steep outer reef slopes, caves and ledges with upside-down orientation), and reproduction (occasional hybrids with C. multifasciata). FishBase retains the genus placement Centropyge venusta (synonym Holacanthus venustus).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used to confirm distribution (Ryukyu and Izu Islands, Taiwan, northern Luzon), max size (12 cm), genus/subgenus history (Paracentropyge sometimes treated as distinct genus), aquarium trade scarcity, captive-breeding success, and IUCN Least Concern status.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; captive rearing has reportedly been achieved but is not routine. Protogynous hermaphrodite, almost certainly haremic.
